denial_letter_text:
Dear Member,

We received your provider's request on 03/15/2026 for Additional psychotherapy visits beyond plan annual limit related to Generalized anxiety disorder (F41.1). After review, we cannot approve this request.

Our review applied UnitedHealthcare Medical Management Guidelines and Optum clinical criteria. The documentation submitted does not demonstrate that the service is medically necessary for this member at this time. Additional psychotherapy visits beyond plan annual limit is not considered evidence-based for Generalized anxiety disorder (F41.1) under current medical policy. Published criteria do not support authorization.

Specifically, the information provided does not satisfy the utilization-management requirements associated with not evidence based. The clinical notes reviewed did not include sufficient detail to confirm that plan criteria were met. The requesting provider may submit additional records, but the current file does not support approval.

If your provider wishes to discuss this determination, they may contact our physician review line. Any discussion must occur within the timeframe described in your plan materials.

You have the right to appeal this determination through our internal appeals process. To file a standard appeal, send a written request and supporting medical records within 180 days of the date of this letter. Include your member identification number and the service reference shown on your explanation of benefits.

Sincerely,

UnitedHealthcare Utilization Management
clinical_context: The 27-year-old member has Generalized anxiety disorder (F41.1). He has been treated under commercial coverage with Additional psychotherapy visits beyond plan annual limit recommended as the next appropriate step. GAD-7 remained 16 after 16 sessions; prior auth supported weekly therapy for acute worsening in March 2026. This directly contradicts the insurer's position that not evidence based bars coverage: the chart documents dates, doses, and objective findings that were submitted with the original request submitted in Q1 2026 but are not reflected in the denial letter's rationale. Treating clinicians note ongoing functional impairment and avoidable harm if his care is delayed further.
